title: Labor shelves private hospital pricing shake-up plan
full_text:
Labor has shelved a controversial proposal to introduce a benchmark pricing model for private hospital procedures following opposition from the biggest operator, Ramsay Health Care, to what would have been the biggest shake-up to the private health system in decades.
Instead, the Albanese government will look at changes to private health insurance product tiers, maternity and mental health care and hospitals in the home as part of a public consultation process designed to make the hospital sector more viable and stave off ward closures.
The chief executives of the country’s biggest private hospitals and health insurers met the Department of Health this week but failed to walk away with a solution to growing pressures on the private healthcare system after two years of consultations.
The Australian Private Hospitals Association, an industry body whose members include Ramsay, on Thursday accused the government of failing to make any decisions to help the private healthcare system, and labelled the CEO Forum established two years ago a “dud”.
“After more than two years of talking about a crisis that emerged and deepened on the Albanese government’s watch, these government-led talk fests have failed to deliver anything at all,” APHA chief executive Brett Heffernan said.
The most significant reform proposed by the Department of Health to the CEO Forum last year was introducing a private national efficient price by July 2028, similar to the model used in public hospitals.
It would have replaced the existing combative system where hospitals and health insurers negotiate individual funding deals.
However, that idea is on the backburner after Ramsay and other hospital groups and insurers pushed back.
Ramsay’s lobbying was led by Health Minister Mark Butler’s former chief of staff, Nick Martin, who was appointed the company’s head of government relations in November.
Instead, the government would propose new settings for maternity and mental health care and hospital in the home as part of a public consultation process, said sources familiar with the situation but not authorised to speak publicly.
The consultation will also look at changes to gold, silver, bronze and basic health insurance product tiers, and support for patients in regional Australia.
“In response to the CEO Forum’s feedback, the government will consult more widely with the sector on how we can improve transparency, choice and affordability in private health care for all,” a federal government spokeswoman said.
Butler has also said reducing specialists’ fees will be a key focus of the government’s health policy reform.
A parliamentary inquiry is also looking at the availability, cost and equity of access of medical specialists across Australia.
Consensus hard to find
The private health sector has struggled to find consensus on how to fix an industry struggling with soaring costs and steep pay rises for nurses and other staff.
Catholic operators supported a benchmark price, health insurers had mixed feelings, and Ramsay opposed to the move because it already gets the best prices because of its market power.
Catholic Health Australia, which represents 63 not-for-profit hospitals, said it supported a default benefit for hospital-in-the-home care, measures to simplify private health insurance products, changes to funding and making data more transparent.
“There are real short-term wins here we can deliver for patients and hospitals now, and getting them done builds the platform for the long-term reform the sector needs,” CHA acting chief executive Katharine Bassett said.
“For too long, private hospitals have been constrained by an opaque and inefficient contracting system that favours powerful insurers and risks the future delivery of essential health care services.”
Private hospital operators want private health insurers to increase funding, while health funds argue many operators are inefficient and have failed to keep up with changing clinical needs.
Private maternity and mental health wards have been the hardest hit due to workforce shortages, fewer women having babies, and psychiatrists preferring to treat patients in out-of-hospital settings.
Butler conducted a review of the private hospital system last year which found cost growth was outstripping revenue growth. However, he has indicated the government has no appetite to directly bail out private operators.
Healthscope, the country’s second-largest operator, fell into receivership last year with debts of $1.6 billion.
A proposal to turn the company into a not-for-profit entity seeks to avoid hospital closures, while a consortium of private buyers is also looking at the company.
